questions.text: What does the Latin term 'Uberrima Fides' mean in the context of insurance contracts?
questions.explanation: Uberrima Fides means Utmost Good Faith. It is a fundamental principle requiring both the insurer and the insured to transparently disclose all material facts before the contract is signed.

questions.text: In the principal-agent relationship under insurance law, who is the 'Principal'?
questions.explanation: The Insurance Company is the Principal, and the agent is the authorized representative who acts on behalf of the company to solicit business.

questions.text: What is the maximum time limit for an Insurance Ombudsman to pass an award after receiving all required documents?
questions.explanation: The Insurance Ombudsman is mandated to resolve the dispute and pass a binding award within 3 months of receiving all necessary paperwork from the complainant.

questions.text: According to IRDAI regulations, what is the minimum age requirement to become a licensed insurance agent?
questions.explanation: An individual must have completed 18 years of age to apply for an insurance agency license in India.

questions.text: As per Section 45 of the Insurance Act (Indisputability Clause), after how many years can an insurer NOT question a life policy on any grounds?
questions.explanation: No policy of life insurance can be called into question on any ground whatsoever after the expiry of 3 years from the date of policy issuance or revival.

questions.text: If a policyholder commits suicide within 12 months of policy inception, what does the nominee receive under standard policy terms?
options: 80% of the total premiums paid till the date of death
questions.explanation: The standard suicide clause dictates that if suicide occurs within the first 12 months, the nominee is refunded 80% of the premiums paid (excluding taxes).

questions.text: What is the 'Free Look Period' for an insurance policy bought through distance marketing (e.g., online or telemarketing)?
questions.explanation: While the regular Free Look Period is 15 days, policies purchased via electronic mode or distance marketing enjoy an extended 30-day Free Look Period.

questions.text: What is a unique tax benefit of Keyman Insurance for the employer?
options: The premium paid is treated as a deductible business expense
No tax is levied on the policy ever
questions.explanation: For the company buying Keyman Insurance, the premiums paid are treated as a legitimate business expense under Section 37(1) of the Income Tax Act, reducing their taxable profit.

questions.text: Under which section of the Married Women's Property (MWP) Act is a trust created to protect the life insurance policy from creditors?
questions.explanation: Section 6 of the MWP Act provides that a policy taken by a married man on his own life for the benefit of his wife/children becomes a trust and cannot be attached by his creditors.

questions.text: Which of the following is an example of a 'Non-Standard Age Proof'?
questions.explanation: Horoscopes, self-declarations, and elder's declarations are non-standard proofs. Insurers may accept them but usually impose a restriction or extra premium.

questions.text: What does a 'Survival Benefit' mean in a Money Back policy?
options: Periodic lump sum payouts made to the surviving policyholder at pre-defined intervals during the policy term
questions.explanation: Money Back policies provide regular liquidity through survival benefits (e.g., 20% of sum assured every 5 years) as long as the policyholder is alive.

questions.text: In a Unit Linked Insurance Plan (ULIP), what does 'NAV' stand for?
questions.explanation: Net Asset Value (NAV) represents the price of a single unit of the chosen investment fund in a ULIP. It fluctuates daily based on market performance.

questions.text: What is the standard 'Grace Period' for a policy with a half-yearly premium payment mode?
questions.explanation: The grace period is 30 days for Yearly, Half-yearly, and Quarterly premium payment modes. For monthly modes, it is 15 days.

questions.text: What is the legal implication of 'Absolute Assignment' of a life insurance policy?
options: It completely and permanently transfers all rights, title, and ownership to the assignee
It temporarily pledges the policy for a loan
questions.explanation: Absolute assignment is an irrevocable transfer of ownership. The assignee becomes the absolute owner and can even surrender the policy without asking the original owner.

questions.text: Which concept allows a policyholder to continue their life cover at a reduced sum assured even if they stop paying premiums after a few years?
questions.explanation: If a traditional policy has acquired a surrender value and the premium stops, it is automatically converted into a Paid-up policy, continuing the life cover at a proportionately reduced amount.

questions.text: What is an 'Early Claim' in life insurance?
options: A claim made before paying the first premium
A claim arising within 3 years of the policy inception or its revival
A claim made by a minor
questions.explanation: Claims occurring within 3 years are termed Early Claims. They are scrutinized heavily by insurers to rule out non-disclosure of facts or fraud at the time of proposal.

questions.text: Under IRDAI's Micro-Insurance regulations, what is the maximum life insurance cover (Sum Assured) allowed?
questions.explanation: Micro-insurance is designed for the low-income sector. To keep it simple and affordable, the maximum life cover allowed under a micro-insurance policy is capped at Rs. 2 Lakhs.

questions.text: In a Group Insurance scheme, who receives the 'Master Policy'?
options: The Employer or the head of the group
questions.explanation: The employer/group administrator signs the contract and holds the Master Policy, while individual members receive a Certificate of Insurance.

questions.text: What is the primary purpose of KYC (Know Your Customer) documents in insurance?
options: To prevent money laundering and establish the true identity and address of the customer
questions.explanation: KYC is a mandatory Anti-Money Laundering (AML) measure to ensure that illegal funds are not funneled into the financial system through fake identities.

questions.text: What are the three core elements used by actuaries to calculate life insurance premiums?
options: Agent Commission, Office Rent, and Taxes
questions.explanation: Premiums are calculated based on the expected Mortality rate, the Interest the company expects to earn by investing the premium, and the Expenses of running the business.

questions.text: What is the primary goal of the Underwriting process?
options: To fairly assess the risk profile of the applicant and charge an appropriate premium, preventing anti-selection
To maximize the company's stock value
questions.explanation: Underwriting ensures equity. It evaluates whether an applicant is a standard or sub-standard risk, ensuring they pay a premium that accurately reflects their risk of claim.

questions.text: Which of the following describes a 'Moral Hazard'?
options: An applicant hiding a severe drinking problem and applying for a disproportionately high sum assured
questions.explanation: Moral hazard involves subjective risks related to the applicant's character, honesty, or intentions, such as hiding facts to defraud the insurer.

questions.text: What is the 'Solvency Margin' required for insurance companies?
options: The maximum profit they can make
The required excess of assets over liabilities (usually 150%) to ensure the company can pay all claims even in worst-case scenarios
The minimum number of agents they must hire
The maximum premium they can charge
questions.explanation: IRDAI mandates a 150% Solvency Ratio, meaning an insurer must have Rs. 150 in assets for every Rs. 100 in liabilities, guaranteeing financial stability.

questions.text: What is 'Reinsurance'?
options: The process where an insurance company transfers a portion of its assumed risk to another insurance company
questions.explanation: Reinsurance is 'insurance for insurers.' It helps direct insurers manage huge risks by spreading the liability to global reinsurance companies.

questions.text: In a pension plan, what is the 'Vesting Age'?
options: The age when the policy is bought
The age when the accumulation phase ends and the regular pension (annuity) payouts begin
The age when the person dies
questions.explanation: The vesting date is the predetermined milestone in a deferred annuity plan when the invested corpus vests, and the insurer starts paying the regular pension.

questions.text: Why do life insurance companies use the 'Level Premium' system?
options: To ensure the premium increases every year
To average out the increasing cost of mortality over the term, so the customer pays the same fixed premium every year
To decrease the premium in old age
To link premiums to the stock market
questions.explanation: The level premium system charges a bit more in the early years to build a reserve, which subsidizes the much higher mortality risk in the later years, keeping the premium constant.

questions.text: What does 'Surrender Value' represent?
options: The guaranteed cash amount a policyholder receives if they voluntarily terminate a savings-oriented policy before maturity
questions.explanation: If a customer exits a traditional policy early, the insurer pays back the accumulated savings portion of the premiums as the Surrender Value.

questions.text: Under Section 80C of the Income Tax Act, what is the maximum total deduction allowed per financial year for investments, including life insurance premiums?
questions.explanation: Taxpayers can claim a maximum aggregate deduction of Rs. 1.5 Lakhs under Section 80C for life insurance premiums, PPF, ELSS, etc.

questions.text: Under Section 10(10D) of the Income Tax Act, what is the condition for a maturity claim to be completely tax-free (for policies issued after April 2012)?
options: The policy must be a term plan
The annual premium must not exceed 10% of the base Sum Assured
The policyholder must be a senior citizen
The policy must be a ULIP
questions.explanation: To qualify for tax exemption under Section 10(10D), the premium paid in any year must not exceed 10% of the actual capital sum assured.

questions.text: What is the primary function of the IGMS portal established by IRDAI?
options: To provide a central platform for policyholders to register and track insurance grievances against any company
questions.explanation: The Integrated Grievance Management System (IGMS) ensures transparency and accountability by allowing customers to track the status of their complaints directly with the regulator.

questions.text: Which document is considered the definitive evidence of the insurance contract?
questions.explanation: The Policy Bond is the final, legally binding contract that contains the schedule, standard terms, conditions, privileges, and exclusions of the insurance coverage.

questions.text: What does the issuance of the First Premium Receipt (FPR) indicate?
options: The insurer has accepted the risk, and the insurance contract has officially commenced
questions.explanation: The FPR is the conclusive evidence that the premium has been accepted and the risk cover has actively begun.

questions.text: Which principle defines that a person can only buy insurance on a life if they would suffer a financial loss upon that person's death?
questions.explanation: Insurable Interest ensures insurance isn't used for gambling. You can insure your spouse or yourself, but you cannot insure a stranger because you have no financial stake in their life.

questions.text: Why does the 'Principle of Indemnity' NOT apply to Life Insurance?
options: Because human life cannot be accurately measured in exact financial terms, so a life policy pays a pre-agreed guaranteed amount
Because life policies have no surrender value
questions.explanation: Indemnity means restoring the exact financial loss (like replacing a crashed car). You cannot 'replace' a life, so life insurance is a 'Benefit' contract, not an 'Indemnity' contract.

questions.text: What is the unethical practice of 'Churning' or 'Twisting'?
options: An agent convincing a customer to surrender a good existing policy just to buy a new one, solely to generate fresh commission
questions.explanation: Churning harms the customer because they lose the accumulated benefits of their old policy, while the agent illegally profits from a new first-year commission.

questions.text: What is 'Need Analysis' in the insurance sales process?
options: Analyzing the agent's need for commission
The ethical process of evaluating a prospect's financial situation, goals, and liabilities to recommend the most suitable product
questions.explanation: Need analysis is the core of ethical selling. It ensures the customer buys a policy that fits their specific life stage (e.g., child education, retirement, pure protection).

questions.text: What is the primary role of a TPA (Third Party Administrator) in Health Insurance?
options: To act as an intermediary that processes health claims and manages cashless hospital networks for the insurer
questions.explanation: TPAs handle the backend administration of health policies, issuing ID cards, approving cashless hospitalizations, and settling reimbursement claims.

questions.text: What is a 'Waiting Period' for Pre-Existing Diseases (PED) in Health Insurance?
options: The time taken to issue the policy
A specific duration (usually 2 to 4 years) during which medical expenses related to a disease the insured had before buying the policy are not covered
The time spent in the waiting room of a hospital
questions.explanation: Waiting periods protect insurers from anti-selection by ensuring people don't buy health insurance just before a planned surgery for an existing ailment.

questions.text: What does a 'Co-payment' clause in a health insurance policy mean?
options: The agent pays half the premium
The insured agrees to pay a fixed percentage of the admissible claim amount out of their own pocket, while the insurer pays the rest
questions.explanation: Co-payment is a cost-sharing method. If there's a 10% co-pay on a 1 Lakh bill, the insured pays 10k, and the insurer pays 90k. This reduces the premium cost.

questions.text: What does an 'e-Insurance Account' (eIA) do?
options: Provides a secure electronic portfolio to hold and manage all life and non-life insurance policies digitally without physical paperwork
questions.explanation: Managed by Insurance Repositories, an eIA digitizes policies, making it easier for customers to track renewals, change addresses, and prevent the loss of physical bonds.

questions.text: What is a 'Prospectus'?
options: A formal document published by the insurer that outlines all the features, benefits, costs, and exclusions of an insurance product
questions.explanation: A prospectus is essentially the official brochure of the policy. Insurers are legally required to provide it so customers understand exactly what they are buying.

questions.text: What does 'Distance Marketing' refer to?
options: Selling policies without face-to-face interaction, using telephone, internet, SMS, or email
questions.explanation: Distance marketing covers all non-physical sales channels. Because the customer can't see the agent, IRDAI gives a longer 30-day free look period for these policies.

questions.text: What does an 'Ex Gratia' payment mean?
options: A voluntary payment made by the insurance company out of goodwill or compassion, even when the claim is not legally payable under the policy terms
questions.explanation: Ex Gratia translates to 'by favor'. It is usually a partial payment given on humanitarian grounds in border-line cases where the strict legal claim is rejected.

questions.text: Which document acts as the final receipt confirming that the insurance company has settled the claim and is released from further liability?
questions.explanation: The Discharge Voucher is a legal acknowledgment signed by the claimant stating that they have received the claim money in full and final settlement of the contract.

questions.text: In a ULIP, what is 'Premium Redirection'?
options: Refunding the premium to the customer
Changing the allocation of *future* premiums to different investment funds without moving the existing accumulated money
Paying the premium to a different company
Taking a loan from the fund
questions.explanation: Premium redirection allows a policyholder to direct upcoming premium installments into a safer fund (like Debt), while keeping past investments in their current funds (like Equity).

questions.text: What happens to the policy if a policyholder dies during the 'Grace Period'?
options: The insurer pays the full sum assured but deducts the unpaid due premium from the final claim amount
questions.explanation: The policy remains fully in force during the grace period. The death claim is honored, but the pending premium is recovered from the payout.

questions.text: What does a 'Critical Illness Rider' provide?
options: A guaranteed lump sum payment immediately upon the diagnosis of a specified severe disease (like cancer or heart attack)
questions.explanation: Unlike standard health indemnity policies, a CI rider pays out the full cover amount in one go upon diagnosis, helping the family deal with income loss and expensive treatments.

questions.text: What is an 'Endorsement' in an insurance policy?
options: An official written alteration or addition attached to the policy document to record changes like address updates or a new nominee
questions.explanation: Any legal change to the standard printed policy bond (like a change of name after marriage) is validated by issuing an Endorsement.

questions.text: Who bears the investment risk in a traditional 'Endowment Policy'?
questions.explanation: Because traditional endowment policies offer guaranteed benefits, the insurance company bears all the risk of investing the premiums in the market to ensure they meet those guarantees.

questions.text: What is the primary characteristic of 'Pure Term Insurance'?
options: Maximum life cover at the lowest premium, with no maturity or survival benefits
questions.explanation: Term insurance is the purest form of life protection. It has no savings component, meaning 100% of the premium goes towards covering the risk of death, making it highly affordable.
